§5.3 Independence, Basis, Dimensions

Recall the definition of independence from preliminaries, see Definition 2.1.1.

Notice that if the zero vector is in the set of vectors, then they must not be independent.

Consider the matrix , with columns the set of vectors , when the number of independent vectors is greater than the dimensions of the vectors, we have free variables, giving a non-zero null space for . Therefore, we have the following system on dependency.

Theorem 5.3.1.

The columns of a matrix are independent iff the null space of only consists of the zero vector (rank = ), while they are dependent otherwise (rank<).

Definition 5.3.2.

A basis of a vector space is a set of vectors that are independent and span the space.

Example 5.3.3.

is as basis of .

Theorem 5.3.4.

The columns of an matrix are a basis of iff is invertible.

Proof.

is invertible means its column vectors are independent. Also, independent vectors of dimensions span .

Definition 5.3.5.

Fix a vector space, define its dimension to be the number of vectors there are in its basis.

Since for a space, the number of vectors its basis has is fixed, its dimension is well-defined (as independent equations solve for variables). Thus, the rank of a matrix is the number of its pivot columns, which is the dimension of its column space.

While the dimension of the null space is the number of free variables.
